message.content: Adversaries may downgrade the operating system of network devices to an older version, thereby weakening security. Older OS versions often have weaker encryption and fewer defensive features. This technique typically involves replacing the OS file in storage and reconfiguring the device to boot from this file upon restart. The adversary can either restart the device immediately or wait for the next scheduled restart. The primary impact is the evasion of defenses, potentially enabling behaviors like weakened encryption. Common exploitation methods include downloading and installing an older OS version and reconfiguring the boot settings. Mitigation strategies include regularly updating and patching network devices, monitoring for unauthorized changes to system images, and employing robust access controls to prevent unauthorized modifications.
